text: Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon identifies a defect in the posterior left diaphragm, with the colon, omentum, and spleen visible within the chest cavity. The stomach is also identified and carefully reduced from the chest back into the abdomen. The surgeon uses electric cautery to remove adhesions from the diaphragm and free the hernia edges circumferentially. The defect, measuring five by seven centimeters, is closed using O-tychron interrupted mattress sutures, with barred felt cut into one by two centimeter rectangles used as pledges.

A seven French drain is placed into the thoracic cavity before the defect is completely closed. The most posterior aspect of the defect is sutured close to the ribs using three separate interrupted sutures, which are pulled through the abdominal wall fascia and tied externally. A stab incision is made subxiphoid, and an endoclose device is used to pull up the free limb of the suture. The exteriorized suture is secured with a clamp, and the endoclose is passed again through the same incision at an angle, incorporating about one centimeter of abdominal wall between the sutures. The suture is then tightened to create tension on the defect and tied down.

A 15x20 cm dual-sided barred mesh with an echo positioning system is selected to reinforce the defect closure. The left lobe of the liver is mobilized, retracting its attachments to the midline, and the peritoneal reflection of the descending colon at the splenic flexure is mobilized to accommodate the mesh. The mesh is introduced into the abdominal cavity through the twelfth port and centered on the most posterior aspect of the defect. It is secured to the diaphragm and abdominal wall circumferentially with 2-0 V-lock sutures, and the central portion is fixated to the diaphragm closure using the same sutures.
